coredata.dc:title: Lucigenin fluorescent assay of tyrosinase activity and its inhibitor screening
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Xiangui
Gao, Wenyue
Halawa, Mohamed Ibrahim
Lan, Yixiang
Li, Jianping
Xu, Guobao
coredata.dc:description: Tyrosinase is a key enzyme in melanin biosynthesis and its serum level is an important biomarker for some dermatoses such as vitiligo and melanoma. Thus, a sensitive and facile assay for tyrosinase activity is in urgent demands. Lucigenin is a well-known chemiluminescent luminophore, but has been rarely been employed for fluorescence detection. In this work, a facile method to detect tyrosinase activity based on lucigenin fluorescence has been reported for the first time. When tyrosinase was mixed with tyrosine, tyrosine was oxidized to generate melanin-like polymers which can effectively quench the fluorescence of lucigenin. A linear range of tyrosinase from 0.25 to 25 μg/mL was obtained with detection limit of 0.2 μg/mL. In addition, a useful platform for screening potential tyrosinase inhibitors was constructed based on the proposed system. The linear range is from 2.5 to 62.5 μg/mL and the detection limit is as low as 1.0 μg/mL for kojic acid, a typical tyrosinase inhibitor.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tyrosinase
Lucigenin
Kojic acid
Fluorescent biosensor
Inhibitors
Drug screening

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of tyrosinase in human serum samples
Quenching of lucigenin fluorescence by melanin-like polymers generated from tyrosinase-catalyzed oxidation of tyrosine
Selectivity of the proposed lucigenin based fluorescent assay
Determination of tyrosinase in serum sample

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of fluorescence quenching strategy for tyrosinase activity detection.
Fluorescence emission spectra under different conditions. Tyrosine 2.5 mM; tyrosinase 5 μg/mL; incubation time 60 min at 37 ℃, 0.1 M PBS, pH 7.4, lucigenin 5 μM.

Fluorescence lifetime decay curves of lucigenin (blue dots) and lucigenin mixed with tyrosinase and tyrosine (red dots). Tyrosine 2.5 mM; tyrosinase 5 μg/mL; incubation time 60 min at 37 ℃, 0.1 M PBS, pH 7.4, lucigenin 1 μM. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Kinetic measurements of fluorescence responses. Tyrosine 2.5 mM, tyrosinase 2.0 μg/mL, lucigenin 5.0 μM, 0.1 M PBS, pH 7.4.
Optimization of tyrosine concentration. Incubation time 60 min at 37 ℃, tyrosinase 2.0 μg/mL, lucigenin 5.0 μM, PBS pH 7.4.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of lucigenin with various concentrations of tyrosinase (incubation time 60 min at 37 ℃, tyrosine 2.5 mM, lucigenin 5.0 μM, 0.1 M PBS pH 7.4); (B) The corresponding fluorescence intensity at 500 nm versus the concentrations of tyrosinase. The inset was the linear relationship between the quenching efficiency (F0/F) and the concentrations of tyrosinase.
Selective fluorescence response of the proposed system with 250.0 μM of Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe3+; 1.0 mg/mL of BSA; 10.0 U/mL of GOx, trypsin and tyrosinase.

The inhibition analysis of kojic acid on tyrosinase activity. The concentrations of kojic acid were 0, 0.625, 2.50, 6.25, 12.50, 18.75, 37.50, 62.50, 125.00, 250.00, and 500.00 μg/mL. Incubation time 60 min at 37 ℃, tyrosinase 12.5 μg/mL, tyrosine 2.5 mM, lucigenin 5 μM, 0.1 M PBS, pH 7.4.
Comparison of some previously reported assays for tyrosinase detection.
Application of the developed method for determination of tyrosinase in serum sample.

head.ce:title: Lucigenin fluorescent assay of tyrosinase activity and its inhibitor screening

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, Jilin 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei, Anhui 230026, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, No. 19A Yuquanlu, Beijing 100049, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tyrosinase is a key enzyme in melanin biosynthesis and its serum level is an important biomarker for some dermatoses such as vitiligo and melanoma. Thus, a sensitive and facile assay for tyrosinase activity is in urgent demands. Lucigenin is a well-known chemiluminescent luminophore, but has been rarely been employed for fluorescence detection. In this work, a facile method to detect tyrosinase activity based on lucigenin fluorescence has been reported for the first time. When tyrosinase was mixed with tyrosine, tyrosine was oxidized to generate melanin-like polymers which can effectively quench the fluorescence of lucigenin. A linear range of tyrosinase from 0.25 to 25 μg/mL was obtained with detection limit of 0.2 μg/mL. In addition, a useful platform for screening potential tyrosinase inhibitors was constructed based on the proposed system. The linear range is from 2.5 to 62.5 μg/mL and the detection limit is as low as 1.0 μg/mL for kojic acid, a typical tyrosinase inhibitor.

ce:section.ce:para: Tyrosinase, a copper-containing oxidase, widely exists in all kinds of organisms from plants to mammals [1,2]. Tyrosinase mainly participates in two pivotal reactions of melanin synthesis. It can catalyze the oxidation of monophenols to o-diphenols and the further oxidation of o-diphenols to corresponding o-quinones in the presence of molecular oxygen. These o-quinones undergo several reactions to generate melanin eventually [3]. These reactions are key processes in the biosynthesis of natural pigments, which are responsible for food browning reactions [4] and some human dermatoses including albinism, vitiligo and skin hyperpigmentation [5]. Thus, highly effective inhibitors of tyrosinase are urgently in demand to prevent the unfavorable darkening phenomena mentioned above. These tyrosinase inhibitors are widely used in treatment of cutaneous hyperpigmentation, cosmetic industry and food industry for preventing enzymatic browning [6,7]. Additionally, because of its effect on dopamine neurotoxicity, the imbalance of tyrosinase in the human body may cause some neural disorders such as Parkinson’s syndrome [8,9]. Moreover, tyrosinase is regarded as a dependable biomarker for melanoma cancer [10,11] owing to its elevated expressing level in melanoma cancer cells. Therefore, it is of great importance to develop a facile and novel method to detect tyrosinase activity and seek new potent inhibitors for both fundamental research and practical application.

Up to now, there are various published works for the detection of tyrosinase activity, including colorimetric [12,13], electrochemical [14,15] and fluorescent [16–23] methods. Although conventional colorimetric approaches for tyrosinase sensing are fast and straightforward, they suffer from low sensitivity and selectivity. While fluorescent methods still draw major attention because of its stability and high sensitivity. In recent years, molecular probes [16–18], quantum dots [19–21] and functionalized nanoparticles [22,23] have been employed for tyrosinase assay. However, these fluorescent materials generally need laborious synthesis procedures and time-consuming modification processes.

Lucigenin (N,N-dimethyl-9,9′-biacridinium nitrate) is an acridine-based chemiluminescence probe that has been wildly adopted to detect reactive oxygen species (ROS) [24]. Furthermore, it can emit bright fluorescence light at low concentration, which means that it has low requirement to the apparatus. Lucigenin is a commercially available reagent, and it is characterized by its excellent chemical stability in aqueous solution. All these advantages above make it promising in sensitive analyses. Surpisingly, lucigenin has seldom been used for fluorescent sensing.

In this study, we developed a facile and novel fluorescent method to detect tyrosinase activity (Scheme 1 ). Tyrosinase catalyzed the oxidation of tyrosine by the dissolved oxygen into dopamine quinine that can generate melanin-like polymers [23,25] after incubation. Previous reports prove that melanin-like polymers such as polydopamine [26] have excellent fluorescent quench ability. And the fluorescence of lucigenin could be effectively quenched by the oxidation products, which was employed for the detection of tyrosinase activity. Furthermore, the proposal sensing system was also used to screening tyrosinase inhibitors.

Lucigenin was obtained from TCI (Shanghai, China). Tyrosinase and glucose oxidase (GOx) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. CuSO4 and Zn(NO3)2 were obtained from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company (Beijing, China). Kojic acid and Fe2(SO4)3 was bought from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Trypsin was obtained from Beijing Dingguo Changsheng Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Tyrosine was bought from Shanghai Yuanju Bio-Tech Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tyrosinase was dissolved in 0.05 M PBS buffer (pH 6.5). All reagents were of analytical grade, and used directly throughout the experiments without any further purification. Doubly distilled water was used through all the experiments.

A RF-5301PC model spectrophotofluorimeter (Shimadzu, Japan), equipped with a 150 W Xenon arc lamp, was used for the measurement of fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence emission spectra were measured at room temperature with an excitation wavelength of 370 nm, and then the fluorescence intensity at 500 nm was recorded for the analysis. The slit widths for excitation spectra and emission spectra were 3.0 nm and 5.0 nm respectively. The fluorescence lifetime was measured by Modular FluoroLog–3 spectrofluorimeter (Horiba Jobin Yvon) with a Nd: YAG laser diode as the excitation source and a R5509-72 PMT as detector. A 1.0 mm quartz cuvette was used in both fluorescence and absorbance measurements.

Aliquots of 40.0 μL of 100.0 mM tyrosine solution were added to 360.0 μL of PBS buffer (pH 7.4, 0.1 M) and mixed thoroughly with 400.0 μL tyrosinase solution of different concentration into a 5.0 mL microcentrifugal tube. After incubation at 37 ℃ for 60 min, aliquots of 200.0 μL of the above mixture solution were added to 160 μL of PBS buffer (pH 7.4, 0.1 M). Subsequently, 40.0 μL aliquots of 50 μM lucigenin were mixed with the resultant reaction mixture together at room temperature thoroughly. The final solution contains 2.5 mM tyrosine and 5 μM lucigenin. The fluorescence intensity of this solution was recorded at 500 nm with excitation wavelength at 370 nm. Furthermore, the selective fluorescence response of the proposed system with Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe3+ (250 μM); BSA(1 mg/mL); GOx, trypsin, tyrosinase (10 U/mL) was measured respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of tyrosinase in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human blood samples, donated by healthy volunteers from Jilin University First Hospital, were used to assess the feasibility of the proposed assay in the real example application. After 10.0 min of ultra-centrifugation at 12,000 rpm at room temperature, the supernatant of blood was used as serum sample. The proposed fluorescence method was applied to detect the tyrosinase activity in the human serum samples (1%, diluted with 0.1 M PBS).

We injected 200 μL of various concentrations of Kojic acid solutions (dissolved in 0.1 M PBS buffer, pH 7.4) into tyrosinase solutions, and incubated for 5 min. Then, tyrosine solution added to incubate at 37 ℃ for 60 min. Finally, lucigenin was added to measure the fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching of lucigenin fluorescence by melanin-like polymers generated from tyrosinase-catalyzed oxidation of tyrosine
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 1 illustrates the strategy of lucigenin based fluorescent sensing method for the detection of tyrosinase activity. To test the feasibility of the developed fluorescent assay for tyrosinase activity, the fluorescence intensity of lucigenin solutions under different conditions were tested (Fig. 1 ). When tyrosine or tyrosinase was incubated for 60 min at 37 ℃ separately, the fluorescence intensity of lucigenin did not exhibit appreciable changes compared with the control sample of lucigenin. In contrast, when tyrosine and tyrosinase were added in the same time, a significant decrease in lucigenin fluorescence intensity was observed, suggesting that this effective quenching of the fluorescence of lucigenin was ascribed to a tyrosine oxidation reaction under the catalysis of tyrosinase. Therefore, this method could be used as a simple platform to detect tyrosinase activity.

To further investigate the mechanism of the fluorescence quenching, we took the time–resolved fluorescence decay measurements. As shown in Fig. 2 , the fluorescence decay of lucigenin was recorded in the absence and presence of melanin-like polymers. Emission wavelength was at 500 nm, and excitation wavelength was at 370 nm. The average decay lifetime before and after the addition of melanin-like polymers were calculated to be 11.27 ns and 10.87 ns separately using a 2-exponential decay fit. Since no significant difference between these average decay lifetimes, the fluorescence quenching can be mainly ascribed to the static quenching effect. There are reports that melanin-like polymers show negative charge in the neutral environment [27,28]. So we inferred that, when lucigenin was mixed with this tyrosinase reaction product, lucigenin dication can be electrostatically adsorbed by the melanin-like polymers, resulting in intense quenching effect for lucigenin fluorescence.

To improve the sensitivity of this developed method, reaction conditions such as the concentration of tyrosine and incubation time were optimized. Since the fluorescence intensity was strongly affected by reaction time, the time optimization was explored in the phosphate buffer of pH 7.4 at 37 ℃. As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence intensity at 500 nm decreased significantly from 10 min to 60 min incubation, and then the downtrend slowed down after 60 min. Thus, incubation time of 60 min was adopted in the following steps.

The effect of tyrosine concentration was investigated for tyrosinase detection with incubation time of 60 min at 37 ℃. Fig. 4 shows that when the concentration of tyrosine is up to 2.5 mM, the fluorescence signals of lucigenin at 500 nm changed slightly, indicating that the generating melanin-like polymers reached the maximum value. Therefore, tyrosine concentration of 2.5 mM was chosen in the following procedures.

Under the optimal conditions, a facile strategy for quantitative assays of tyrosinase was developed based on lucigenin fluorescence quenching. Different concentrations of tyrosinase were mixed with tyrosine to incubate for 60 min at 37 ℃. Then lucigenin solution was introduced to measure the fluorescence intensity.

As shown in Fig. 5 , upon addition of serial increasing tyrosinase concentration, the fluorescence intensity of the reaction mixture at 500 nm decreased. Fig. 5B shows the linear correlation between the quenching efficiency (F0/F) and the concentration of tyrosinase from 0.25 to 25.0 μg/mL (0.67–67.0 U/mL). As depicted in the inset of Fig. 5B, the regression equation can be described as F0/F = 0.964 + 0.260 C tyrosinase (μg/mL) with a correlation coefficient (r) of 0.9995 (where the F0 and F referred to the fluorescence intensity without and with tyrosinase, respectively). The detection limit for tyrosinase is calculated to be 0.20 μg/mL (0.54 U/mL) at a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which is comparable with those methods in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the proposed lucigenin based fluorescent assay
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the selectivity of the constructed sensing system, especially in biomedical detection, some common interfering substances, such as Cu2+, Zn2+, Fe3+, BSA, GOx and trypsin were tested. Fig. 6 illuminates that GOx, BSA and some metal ions like Cu2+, Zn2+ and Fe3+ have no significant interference on tyrosinase detection. Trypsin has slight effect on tyrosinase detection, which probably result from the interaction between trypsin and lucigenin. These data proved that this method has favorable selectivity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of tyrosinase in serum sample
ce:section.ce:para: The performance of this sensing approach in real sample assay was analyzed by detecting tyrosinase in fresh human serum (1%). The recoveries shown in Table 2 were used to depict the results. When 1.0, 5.0, 10.0 μg/mL of tyrosinase were added, the recoveries were measured to be 92.0%–97.8%, indicating that this lucigenin quenching method is reliable with good practicality and promising for the detection of tyrosinase activity in biological samples.

Tyrosinase inhibitors can effectively inhibit the activity of tyrosinase and thereby block the biosynthesis of melanin and browning process. And tyrosinase inhibitors are widely used in skin whitening and food antibrowning [30]. Thus, it is important to develop a convenient and efficient screening method for tyrosinase inhibitors. In this case, kojic acid, an intensively studied tyrosinase inhibitor, was adopted to investigate the screening function of this assay.

Tyrosinase was preincubated with solutions containing different concentrations of kojic acid for 5 min at room temperature. Then tyrosinase was introduced to the tyrosine solution for an incubation at 37 ℃ for 60 min. Finally lucigenin was added, and fluorescence emission spectra were measured (Fig. 7 ). The fluorescence intensity increased with the increase of concentration of kojic acid, which is corresponding with the illustration in Scheme 1. Furthermore, inhibition efficiency (IE%) was used as a signal to detect kojic acid. IE% was defined as the following equation: Inhibition efficiency (IE) = (Finhibitor — Fno inhibitor) / (F0 — Fno inhibitor) × 100%. Finhibitor and Fno inhibitor referred to the fluorescence intensity at 500 nm with and without kojic acid, respectively. F0 referred to the fluorescence intensity of lucigenin in the absence of tyrosinase and kojic acid. As shown in the inset of Fig. 7, a linear correlation between IE(%) and the concentration of kojic acid was established from 2.5 to 62.5 μg/mL (0.018–0.440 mM). The regression equation can be expressed as IE(%) = 3.08 + 1.04 Ckojic acid (μg/mL), R2 = 0.9981. The detection limit of kojic acid is 1.0 μg/mL (7.0 μM), which is comparable to those previously reported tyrosinase activity assays [31–33]. Thus, from the results introduced above, it can be concluded that the proposed method can be used for both tyrosinase activity analysis and the quantitative screening of potential tyrosinase inhibitors.

Herein, we have developed a facile method for tyrosinase activity detection based on lucigenin fluorescence quenching. Compared with other methods based on nanomaterials, our method based on lucigenin is a simple, eco-friendly, cost effective and straightforward approach without the need of tedious prolonged synthetic steps. Under the optimum conditions, the detection limit for tyrosinase activity was as low as 0.54 U/mL. Moreover, this established fluorescence sensing approach provided excellent selectivity, sensitivity and label-free detection, and also showed a promising prospect for the screening of tyrosinase inhibitors.
The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We thank the support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21475123 and 21675148), the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2016YFA0201300), the Chinese Academy of Sciences (CAS)-the Academy of Sciences for the Developing World (TWAS) President’s Fellowship Program.

4-mercaptophenylboronic acid-induced in situ formation of silver nanoparticle aggregates as labels on an electrode surface

A Two-photon fluorescent probe for intracellular detection of tyrosinase activity

A fluorometric biosensor based on functional Au/Ag nanoclusters for real-time monitoring of tyrosinase activity
Melanin-like nanoquencher on graphitic carbon nitride nanosheets for tyrosinase activity and inhibitor assay
Stern–volmer quenching and binding constants of 10-alkyl-9(10H)-acridone probes in SDS and BSA
Polydopamine and eumelanin: from structure–property relationships to a unified tailoring strategy
Two-in-one polydopamine nanospheres for fluorescent determination of beta-amyloid oligomers and inhibition of beta-amyloid aggregation
Protein adsorption on dopamine–melanin films: role of electrostatic interactions inferred from ζ-potential measurements versus chemisorption
On-column enzymatic synthesis of melanin nanoparticles using cryogenic poly(AAM-co-AGE) monolith and its free radical scavenging and electro-catalytic properties

An updated review of tyrosinase inhibitors

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Xiangui Ma obtained his BSc degree from Fuzhou University in 2016. Currently he is a Ph. D. student of Prof. Guobao Xu at the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry of Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, China. His research interests focus on fluorescent and electrochemiluminescent biosensing.

Wenyue Gao received her B. S. degree from Shandong Normal University in 2012. She got her Ph. D. degree under the guidance of Prof. Guobao Xu at Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Her research interests focus on electrochemiluminescence, ion selective electrode, and fluorescence.

Mohamed Ibrahim Halawa received his BSc and MSc from Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University, Egypt. He is assistant lecture at pharmaceutical analytical chemistry department of Faculty of Pharmacy, Mansoura University. Currently he is a Ph. D. student of Prof. Guobao Xu at Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, China. His research focuses on nanocluster-based luminescent sensors, nanomaterial-based bioanalysis, chemiluminescence, chromatographic and capillary electrophoresis techniques.

Yixiang Lan received his B.Eng. degree from Yulin Normal University in 2016. Currently he is studying for a M.S. degree under the guidance of Prof. Guobao Xu and Prof. Jianping Li at Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. His research interests focus on fluorescence sensors.

Jianping Li is a professor in College of Chemistry and Bioengineering, Guilin University of Technology. He received his bachelor and master degree from Jilin University in 1988 and 1991, and a PhD degree from Zhejiang University in 2003. His researches focused on chemical sensor and biosensors.

Guobao Xu received his BSc, MSc, and PhD from Jilin University, Xiamen University, and Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, respectively. He did postdoctoral research at the University of Hong Kong, the Hong Kong Polytechnic University, and NTT/JST. He is a professor of the State Key Laboratory of Electroanalytical Chemistry. His research interests involve electrochemistry, luminescence, synthesis of nanomaterials, biosensing, energy conversion, and devices.
